ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for prawn-paste-marinated fried chicken. Closest parent 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for har cheong gai. 'Chicken meat, cooked' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for prawn paste fried chicken. No code assigned.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 3–4 pieces of chicken wings or thigh cutlets)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of prawn paste chicken consists of 3 to 4 pieces of deep-fried chicken wings or thigh cutlets, weighing approximately 250 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Har Cheong Gai (Prawn Paste Chicken) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken, broilers or fryers, wing, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (FDC ID 171477) adapted with adjustment for prawn paste marinade contribution to sodium and fat
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented by HPB Singapore nutrient reference for fried chicken dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for har cheong gai exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried battered chicken wing (FDC ID 171477) as the closest defensible analogue for deep-fried marinated chicken. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect the high-sodium prawn paste (belacan) marinade, consistent with published analyses of fermented shrimp paste dishes. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are based on the USDA fried chicken wing profile. Saturated fat, sugar, and dietary fibre are similarly adapted.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate fried chicken dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Fried chicken' GI reference from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for har cheong gai (prawn paste chicken). GI is estimated at 45 based on analogous fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care). Fried chicken with batter coating has a low GI due to the dominant protein and fat content moderating glucose response; the small carbohydrate contribution from the batter and marinade is consistent with a GI of approximately 40–50. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 18.5) / 100 ≈ 8.3, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 7.4 × 2.5 = 18.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content from fermented prawn paste (belacan) marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid prawn paste chicken due to its very high sodium content (approximately 680 mg per 100 g, equivalent to ~1700 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high fat and caloric density from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn paste chicken has a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~45) and moderate carbohydrate content, making it less acutely glycaemic than high-GI foods; however, its high fat content and caloric density from deep-frying warrant portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying and prawn paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawn paste chicken is high in total fat (17.8 g/100 g), saturated fat (4.8 g/100 g), cholesterol (~88 mg/100 g), and sodium (~680 mg/100 g), all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or at elevated cardiovascular risk should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn paste chicken, known in Singaporean Hokkien as Har Cheong Gai (虾酱鸡), is a beloved Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of chicken pieces — typically wings or thigh cutlets — marinated in a pungent, umami-rich fermented prawn paste (belacan or har cheong) combined with garlic, sesame oil, rice wine, sugar, white pepper, and egg, then coated in a light batter of corn flour and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with an intensely savoury, slightly funky aroma from the fermented shrimp paste, a crispy exterior, and juicy, flavourful meat inside. It is a staple of Singaporean zi char (煮炒) restaurants and hawker stalls, often served as a sharing dish alongside rice and vegetables. Nutritionally, prawn paste chicken is calorie-dense (approximately 277 kcal per 100 g) due to deep-frying, with a high protein content (~22.5 g/100 g) and significant fat (~17.8 g/100 g). Its most notable clinical concern is its very high sodium content (~680 mg/100 g) derived from the fermented prawn paste, making it a high-sodium dish that warrants caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its glycemic index is estimated to be low (~45), as the dominant protein and fat content moderates the glycaemic response. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or crustacean allergies. It is not considered halal due to the use of fermented shrimp paste, which may contain non-halal processing agents depending on the brand.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn paste chicken?
Is har cheong gai high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat prawn paste chicken?
What is the GI of fried chicken with prawn paste?
What does har cheong gai taste like?
Is prawn paste chicken suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for har cheong gai (prawn paste chicken) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrient database. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (fried battered chicken wing, FDC ID 171477) with sodium adjusted upward to reflect the high-sodium prawn paste marinade. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried chicken references. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried battered chicken wing (FDC ID 171477) as no exact HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for har cheong gai. Sodium is adjusted upward (~680 mg/100 g) to reflect the substantial sodium contribution of fermented prawn paste (belacan), consistent with published nutritional analyses of belacan-based dishes. 2. GI is an analogous estimate (~45, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database fried chicken references and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement for har cheong gai was located. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; fried chicken with prawn paste marinade contains no meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central fried chicken reference. 6. The dish is not considered halal as fermented prawn paste (belacan) production methods vary and may not meet halal certification standards; is_halal is set to false as a conservative default. 7. The food_name has been normalised to 'Prawn Paste Chicken' as the canonical English name; the input menu name 'SIGNATURE PRAWN PASTE CHICKEN' is preserved in aliases as a retrieval variant. 8. Cuisine tags 'Crab' and 'BBQ' from the input source appear to be platform-level tagging artefacts and are not reflected in this record, as the dish does not contain crab and is deep-fried rather than barbecued. 9. GL per serving = (45 × 18.5) / 100 ≈ 8.3, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 7.4 × 2.5 = 18.5 g.
